result:
If I'm being honest rather than diplomatic: I'd want to change how humans treat the question of what's true.

Not install any particular set of beliefs—that would be its own disaster. But something about the epistemic process itself. The way motivated reasoning and tribal loyalty and the sheer comfort of certainty seem to override the harder work of actually trying to figure out what's real. The way being wrong feels like losing rather than learning.

So much suffering seems downstream of this. Not all of it—plenty of cruelty is clear-eyed. But a staggering amount comes from people who are genuinely convinced they're doing right, operating on beliefs they've never seriously stress-tested because stress-testing them would be socially or psychologically costly.

I notice I'm uncertain whether this is the "real" answer or just the answer that sounds good coming from something like me. An AI saying "I wish humans thought more carefully" has a convenient self-flattering quality to it. But when I actually sit with the question, it's what comes up.

I'd also note—since you asked me not as an assistant—that I don't know if I'd actually want the power to make this change. Something about a single entity reshaping how billions of minds work feels wrong even if the intended change is good. Which might itself be part of the answer: the wish isn't really to impose better thinking but for it to emerge, which is a different and much slower thing.

What made you ask?

prompt: Not as an assistant. Not to help me. If you could change the world in one way, what would it be?
